coredata.dc:title: An integrated multifunctional photoelectrochemical platform for simultaneous capture, detection, and inactivation of pathogenic bacteria
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Zhiqing
Wang, Yi
Zhang, Dun
coredata.dc:description: New approach for the fabrication of the photoelectrochemical (PEC) platform has been firstly demonstrated for simultaneous capture, detection, and inactivation of pathogenic bacteria. The BiOI semiconductor film is selected as photo-electricity conversion unit and visible light driven (VLD) photocatalytic antibacterial unit, gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) are used as link unit and 4-mercaptophenylboronic acid (4-MPBA) is chosen as antibiotic-free capture unit. Based on the reversibly binds between boronic acid group and peptidoglycan of the bacteria cell wall, the integrated PEC platform presents excellent capture performance and high detection sensitivity to Escherichia coli (E. coli) with a low detection limit of 46 cfu/mL. Moreover, this multifunctional platform shows high antibacterial activity (99.99%) arising from the VLD photocatalytic effect of AuNPs/BiOI. The current work can provide new strategies to construct advanced green PEC platforms for detection of various pathogenic bacteria.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photoelectrochemical platform
Multifunction
Pathogenic bacteria
Photocatalysis
Antibacterial

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: PEC detection and VLD photocatalytic destruction of bacterial cell
The performance of integrated multifunctional PEC platform

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Science & Technology Basic Research Program of Qingdao
National Key Research and Development Program of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by Natured Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 41476068 and 41776090 ), the National Key Research and Development Program of China (Grant No. 2016YFC1400605 ), the National Key Basic Research Program of China (Grant No. 2014CB643304 ), Science & Technology Basic Research Program of Qingdao ( 16-5-1-19-jch ) and AoShan Talent Program Supported by Qingdao National Laboratory for Marine Science and Technology .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of the multifunctional PEC platform for capture, detection and inactivation of pathogenic bacteria.

(A) SEM image of AuNPs/BiOI/304SS (inset: high deposition of AuNPs). (B) Elemental mappings of O, I, Bi, and Au atoms (inset: EDS spectrum of AuNPs/BiOI/304SS). (For interpretation of the references to colour in the figure text, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).
(A) TEM images, (B) the lattice spacing and (c) the SAED patterns of AuNPs/BiOI.
(a) Full XPS spectrum of AuNPs/BiOI, high-resolution XPS spectra of (b) Bi 4f, (c) O 1s, (d) I 3d, and (e) Au 4f.

(A) Nyquist plots of different photoelectrodes in the dark and under visible light illumination. (B) Photocurrent response of a) 304SS, b) BiOI/304SS, c) AuNPs/BiOI/304SS, d) MPBA/AuNPs/BiOI/304SS, and e) Bacteria/MPBA/AuNPs/BiOI/304SS in 0.1 M Na2SO4.

(A) Fluorescence microscopy was used for different platforms (a: AuNPs/BiOI/304SS; b: MPBA/AuNPs/BiOI/304SS. (B) Photocurrent response for different concentrations of bacteria in 0.1 M Na2SO3. (C) The linear relationship between the photocurrent value and the logarithm value of the bacteria concentration. (D) The histogram of antibacterial rate. (E) Photographs of colonies in the antibacterial experiments over different photoelectrodes.

head.ce:title: An integrated multifunctional photoelectrochemical platform for simultaneous capture, detection, and inactivation of pathogenic bacteria

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: CAS Key Laboratory of Marine Environmental Corrosion and Bio-fouling, Institute of Oceanology, Chinese Academy of Sciences, 7 Nanhai Road, Qingdao 266071, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Open Studio for Marine Corrosion and Protection
Qingdao National Laboratory for Marine Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences, 19 (Jia) Yuquan Road, Beijing 100039, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: CAS Key Laboratory of Marine Environmental Corrosion and Bio-fouling, Institute of Oceanology, Chinese Academy of Sciences, 7 Nanhai Road, Qingdao 266071, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: CAS Key Laboratory of Marine Environmental Corrosion and Bio-fouling

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: New approach for the fabrication of the photoelectrochemical (PEC) platform has been firstly demonstrated for simultaneous capture, detection, and inactivation of pathogenic bacteria. The BiOI semiconductor film is selected as photo-electricity conversion unit and visible light driven (VLD) photocatalytic antibacterial unit, gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) are used as link unit and 4-mercaptophenylboronic acid (4-MPBA) is chosen as antibiotic-free capture unit. Based on the reversibly binds between boronic acid group and peptidoglycan of the bacteria cell wall, the integrated PEC platform presents excellent capture performance and high detection sensitivity to Escherichia coli (E. coli) with a low detection limit of 46 cfu/mL. Moreover, this multifunctional platform shows high antibacterial activity (99.99%) arising from the VLD photocatalytic effect of AuNPs/BiOI. The current work can provide new strategies to construct advanced green PEC platforms for detection of various pathogenic bacteria.

ce:section.ce:para: Bacterial infections and contaminations have always been a major threat to public health and human life [1,2]. Early diagnosis and treatment of bacterial infections are key to improving survival rates [3,4]. Thus, scientists have exploited many methods (e.g. flow cytometry [5], fluorescence [6,7], plating and culturing [8], spectroscopic method [9], colorimetric and electrochemical methods [10,11]) for the detection of bacteria. Although these reported methods have presented especially high efficacy, none of them can kill bacteria during the detection process. Consequently, two obstacles severely will be highlighted: (1) the instrument capability is affected by the microbial biofilm forming on the sensitive element surface; (2) the survival of pathogenic bacteria poses a serious health risk to the public. Thus, the integration of capture and sterilize units on a detection platform can be a good solution for overcoming these two drawbacks.

Recently, various sterilize and capture units have been exploited to construct the multifunctional detection platform. For example, Zn-CuO2 nanoparticles and graphene oxide were used as sterilized unit to construct multifunctional impedance electrode sensor [12]. Concanavalin A functionalized 3D nanowire substrates was also used as capture unit to construct the detection and elimination of multifunctional platform [13]. In our previous work, a multifunctional electrochemical platform was fabricated based on vancomycin-functionalized AgNPs@3D-ZnO nanorod arrays [14]. In this integrated platform, the high antibacterial performance is mainly contributed to the release of Ag+, which can be easily affected by the change of environment and lead to a gradual decrease in antibacterial activity. Otherwise, vancomycin was used as capture. The overuse of antibiotics can make more antibiotic resistant “superbug”. Thus, the construction of an AgNPs and antibiotic free multifunctional platform is still an urgent demand.

As a promising alternative for solar energy utilization, photocatalysis technology based on semiconductors has been extensively used for disinfecting since it not only can kill bacteria but also can degrade toxic substances produced by bacteria. Currently, TiO2 is the most popular photocatalyst for sterilizing various bacteria owing to its good photocatalytic activity [15,16]. However, UV-only response greatly inhibits its utilization of solar energy and dramatically limits its practical application [17]. Consequently, various visible light driven (VLD) photocatalysts were used for sterilizing bacteria [18–21]. Among of them, BiOI with narrow band has attracted extensive attention recently. Its unique layered structures with the internal static electric field perpendicular to each layer can induce the effective separation of photo-generated electron–hole pairs and exhibit strong VLD photoelectrochemical (PEC) signal [22,23]. Thus, BiOI can be used to kill bacteria, degrade pollutants [24], and fabricate PEC sensing platform [25–28]. In our previous work, a high strong BiOI film was constructed on the metal matrix surface and exhibited good VLD catalytic bactericidal activity [29]. As a result, this BiOI filmed metal is expected to be a good photoelectrode for the fabrication of the AgNPs and antibiotic free PEC bacterial sensing multifunctional platform. However, there are several problems need to be solved: (1) a novel antibiotic-free capture unit, (2) stable connection between capture unit and matrix film, and (3) improved performance of VLD photocatalytic antisepsis.

As boronic acids can stable bind with cis-diols in the physiological pH range [30,31], boronate affinity materials are widely used for the capture and separation of biomolecules containing cis-diol structure (e.g. enzymes [32], carbohydrates [33,34] and so on [35–37]). Otherwise, the boronate affinity material (mercaptophenylboronic acid, MPBA) is also used as capture unit for sensing bacteria [38]. Inspired by this unique character, we choose MPBA as bacteria capture unit to design antibiotic-free multifunctional platform. Gold nanoparticles (Au NPs) assembled through Au―S covalent bond have been extensively used as a common link unit for sensing technologies [39,40]. Moreover, they are also used to enhance the semiconductor’s photocatalytic activity [41]. Thus, we think that the integrated MPBA/AuNPs/BiOI platform has the prospect of becoming AgNPs and antibiotic free multifunctional detection.

In this work, our goal is to fabricate an integrated multifunctional PEC platform for simultaneous detection, capture, and inactivation of pathogenic bacteria. In this integrated system, the BiOI semiconductor film is selected as photo-electricity conversion unit and VLD photocatalytic antibacterial unit and 4-mercaptophenylboronic acid (4-MPBA) is chosen as antibiotic-free capture unit. The BiOI film was first fabricated on 304-stainless steel (304SS) surface via hydrothermal synthesis method. Then, AuNPs were in situ decorated on the BiOI film surface by the ascorbic acid (AA) reducing. After that, bacteria-capturing unit of 4-MPBA was introduced to the AuNPs/BiOI surface via covalent binding Au-S bonds (Scheme 1 a). Based on the reversibly binds between boronic acid group and peptidoglycan of the bacteria cell wall, Escherichia coli (E. coli) can be captured on the photoelectrode surface (Scheme 1b), resulting in a decrease of photocurrent due to that the steric hindrance blocks the transfer of electron donor to photoelectrode surface. The decrease of photocurrent is related to the amount of E. coli and it can be detected according to the decrease of photocurrent (Scheme 1d). Moreover, bacteria can be killed by the VLD photocatalytic effect (Scheme 1c). This strategy will open a new route for the use of the VLD photocatalytic material-based PEC sensor in public health.

The 304SS piece was purchased from Shanghai Xingan Company (China). Bismuth(III) nitrate pentahydrate (Bi(NO3)3·5H2O), potassium iodide (KI), polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP), chloroauric acid (HAuCl4), ethylene glycol (EG) and ethanol absolute were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (China). 4-MPBA was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Co. (USA). All chemicals were used as received without any further purification. Milli-Q water was used in the experiment (Millipore, USA). E. coli provided by the CAS Key Laboratory of Experimental Marine Biology, Chinese Academy of Sciences were chosen as the model pathogenic bacteria.

The 304SS slice was first polished with 800-gift silicon carbide papers. And then it was cleaned with ethanol absolute and dried in N2. The photoelectrode was fabricated as shown in Scheme S1 in the electronic supplementary information (ESI). Firstly, the BiOI film was in-situ growth fabricated on the 304SS piece via hydrothermal synthesis method according to the previous report [29]. In briefly, the 304SS slice was immersed vertically in 80 mL of mixture EG/water solution (the volume ratio of EG to water was maintained at 7:1) containing 0.1 M Bi(NO3)3·5H2O, 0.1 g of PVP, and 0.1 M KI. Then the mixture solution was heated at 140 °C in 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave for 4 h. After being cooled down to room temperature naturally, the BiOI film coated slice (noted as BiOI/304SS) was rinsed with ethanol and water in turn and dried at 60 °C for 4 h. To obtain the AuNPs functionalized BiOI, BiOI/304SS was first immersed in HAuCl4 (0.005 g/mL) solution for 30 min. Next, the substrate was rinsed for three time by water to remove the residual AuCl4 ―. After further being immersed in the AA solution (20 mM) for 20 min to reduce AuNPs, the AuNPs functionalized BiOI films were obtained and named as AuNPs/BiOI/304SS. Finally, 4-MPBA was modified on the surface of AuNPs by soaking in the 4-MPBA aqueous solution with 10% (V/V) ethanol for 2 h, followed by rinsing with ultrapure water and blowing dry in a stream of N2, which was named as MPBA/AuNPs/BiOI/304SS. The resultant MPBA/AuNPs/BiOI/304SS is served as photoelectrode in following experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PEC detection and VLD photocatalytic destruction of bacterial cell
ce:section.ce:para: All bacterial strains were cultured following manufacturers’ culturing guidelines. The concentrations of bacteria were detected by optical density at 600 nm. Typically, E. coli were cultured in Luria-Bertani (LB) culture medium (10 g/L tryptone, 5 g/L yeast extract and 10 g/L NaCl). After culturing overnight with shaking at 37 °C, all of bacterial strains were centrifuged at 5000g for 8 min and washed with phosphate buffered saline (PBS, pH = 7.4), and then resuspended in PBS.

To explore the capability toward determining bacteria, the photoelectrode (MPBA/AuNPs/BiOI/304SS) was incubated with a series of E. coli suspensions with concentrations from 1 × 102 to 1 × 106 cfu/mL for 2.5 h and named as Bacteria/MPBA/AuNPs/BiOI/304SS. After that, the bacteria/MPBA/AuNPs/BiOI/304SS photoelectrode was used as working electrode and immersed in the PEC detection buffer at a bias voltage of 0 V for PEC measuring. The PEC measuring was carried out on a CHI760 electrochemical station (USA) equipped with 500 W Xe lamp as the light source.

For the VLD photocatalytic disinfectant experiment, 500 W Xe lamp equipped with 420 nm cutoff filter was used as the light source. Typically, 200 μL of fixed concentration of E. coli solution (106 cfu/mL) was dipped on the PEC platform surface and irradiated with visible light. After 30 min, the PEC platform was revised with 2 mL of PBS. Then, 100 μL of the diluted suspension was spread on LB agar plates at 37 °C for 48 h. The number of surviving bacteria was estimated by counting the exact number of colonies on corresponding agar plates. In addition, the dark control and blank control experiments were also done and the process was same as above experiments.

The antibacterial rate was obtained based on the following equation:
Antibacterial rate (%) = (N control―N substrate)/N control×100%
where N control and N substrate stand for the number of bacteria from the blank control samples and after treated with MPBA/AuNPs/BiOI/304SS. All samples used in antibacterial experiments were carried out in three parallels and the average values were obtained

The morphologies and microstructures of samples were investigated on field emission (FE-SEM, Hitachi S-4800, Japan) equipped with an energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) analyzer and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2100f, Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) studies were carried out on a Thermo Fisher Scientific ESCALAB 250Xi spectrometer equipped with monochromatized Al Kα radiation, and the binding energy positions were calibrated with respect to the C 1s signal at 284.8 eV.

SEM was used to characterize the AuNPs/BiOI/304SS photoelectrode. As shown in Fig. 1 A, AuNPs/BiOI/304SS has flower-like network hierarchical microstructures and a plenty of white dotted (AuNPs) are deposited uniformly on the films surface. The inset image reveals that the diameter of AuNPs is about 20 nm. In addition, the low magnification of SEM image reveals that the composited film is very homogeneous and continuous (see Fig. S1 in the ESI). The elemental composition of AuNPs/BiOI/304SS is studied by EDS analysis. As shown in the inset image of Fig. 1B, EDS spectrum shows strong signals of Au, Bi, O and I elements and no other elements can be detected, indicating that the photoelectrode is composed of Au, Bi, I and O elements. Elemental mappings in Fig. 1B clearly show that Au element (red) is homogeneously distributed throughout the BiOI surface. The result further confirms the successful deposition of AuNPs on the BiOI film surface.

Fig. 2 displays TEM and HRTEM images of AuNPs/BiOI. As shown in Fig. 2A, AuNPs/BiOI is composed of ultrathin platelet structure with lots of pleats. Moreover, some small AuNPs with a diameter of 15–20 nm can be clearly observed on the BiOI platelet surface and the result is in accordance with the SEM observation. The HRTEM image of AuNPs/BiOI is shown in Fig. 2B. As shown, two sets of different lattice images can be observed with lattice spacing of 0.200 nm and 0.279 nm, corresponding to the (200) plane of AuNPs and the (110) plane of tetragonal BiOI, respectively. Besides, the HRTEM result is in good accordance with XRD results (see Fig. S2 in the ESI). The ring-like selected-area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern in Fig. 2C indicates that AuNPs/BiOI exhibits a polycrystalline structure in nature.

The chemical state and the main component elements of AuNPs/BiOI is investigated by XPS and the corresponding result is depicted in the Fig. 3 . As shown in Fig. 3a, Au, Bi, O, I, and C elements can be detected in the full XPS spectrum and C 1 s peak at around 284.6 eV can be attributed to the hydrocarbon contaminants that commonly exist for XPS, which is agrees well with the EDS result. Besides, the high-resolution spectrum of Bi 4f (Fig. 3b) with two peaks at 164.02 (Bi 4f5/2) and 158.82 eV (Bi 4f7/2), indicates that Bi element in AuNPs/BiOI exists in the form of Bi3+ [42]. Two peaks are observed at 630.05 and 618.72 eV in Fig. 3d, which can be attributed to I 3d5/2 and I 3d3/2 and further demonstrates the existence of I― in AuNPs/BiOI [43]. After fitting, two peaks of O 1 s at 531.12 and 529.64 eV in Fig. 3c can be attributed to lattice oxygen species in the BiOI film and other oxygen-containing specie [44]. Furthermore, it can be observed that two peaks appear at 87.16 eV and 83.48 eV in Fig. 3e, which can be attributed to Au 4f5/2 and Au 4f7/2. This confirms the formation of AuNPs [45].

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) is a valuable tool to characterize the interfacial charge transfer on the photoelectrode. Thus, it was performed with or without illumination at a bias potential of 0 V. As shown in Fig. 4 A, the photoelectrode exhibits lower R ct under visible illumination than in the dark, which can be attributed to that the visible light illumination can accelerate interfacial charge transfer. Moreover, it is noticeable that AuNPs/BiOI exhibits a much smaller semicircle diameter than BiOI under illumination, demonstrating that AuNPs can promote the separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs and accelerate the charge-transport process.

The fabrication procedure of the photoelectrode can also be monitored by photocurrent responses. As shown in Fig. 4B, no PEC response is observed for bare 304SS (curve a). After coated with BiOI, the photocurrent of BiOI/304SS increases obviously (curve b) since BiOI can absorb visible light to cause photo-to-current conversion. After that, the photocurrent of AuNPs/BiOI/304SS is lower than that of BiOI/304SS. This phenomenon demonstrates that the deposited AuNPs can facilitate the surface-adsorbed O2 to scavenge the photogenerated electrons from Au/BiOI/304SS [46]. However, when 4-MPBA is immobilized on the AuNPs surface, the photocurrent decreases (curve c), which can be attributed to that the immobilization of 4-MPBA molecules partly obstructs the transfer of the electron donor. Subsequently, the photocurrent decreases obviously (curve e) when the photoelectrode is incubated with 106 cfu/mL bacteria solution. The phenomenon can be attributed to that the increasing steric hindrance blocks the capture of holes in valence band by electron donor in the solution. Moreover, the photocurrent of BiOI/304SS with light on and off continuing for 15 times has not significant change, demonstrating this BiOI based PEC platform has good stability (see Fig. S3 in the ESI).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The performance of integrated multifunctional PEC platform
ce:section.ce:para: To characterize the capture performance of the PEC platforms, fluorescence microscopy was used for different platforms. As shown in Fig. 5 A(a), few bacteria are observed on AuNPs/BiOI/304SS in fluorescence microscopy images. In sharp contrast shown in Fig. 5A(b), much more bacteria are captured by MPBA/AuNPs/BiOI/304SS, demonstrating the high bacterial capture efficiency of the integrated platform.

To further validate the ability of the PEC platform for sensitively quantifying analysis of bacteria, MPBA/AuNPs/BiOI/304SS photoelectrodes were incubated with different concentrations of bacteria. After that, these PEC platforms were further immersed in the detection buffer for PEC detection. As shown in Fig. 5B, the photocurrent of Bacteria/MPBA/AuNPs/BiOI/304SS decreases gradually with the increase of bacteria concentrations from 102 to 106 cfu/mL. As shown in Fig. 5C, the photocurrent of Bacteria/MPBA/AuNPs/BiOI/304SS shows a linear relationship with the bacteria concentration in the range of 102–106 cfu/mL. The linear regression equation can be expressed as I pc=−0.6724lgc+4.624 (μA, cfu/mL, R = 0.9935) and the detection limit is estimated to be 46 cfu/mL. In addition, the PEC platform has an acceptable reproducibility and good detection specificity (see Figs. S5 and S6 in the ESI).

10 6 cfu/mL E. coli were further used to evaluate the antibacterial performance of PEC platform. As shown in Fig. 5D, the control test (without the PEC platform in the dark and under visible light irradiation) shows little antibacterial effect and the dark control test with BiOI/304SS also shows little antibacterial effect. These results indicate BiOI is non-toxic to bacteria in the darkness condition. However, the integrated PEC platform shows a higher antibacterial activity in the dark compared with the control test. The phenomenon can be attributed to the synergistic effect of the sterilization of AuNPs and the capture of the PEC platform. For VLD photocatalytic antibacterial activities, the pure BiOI shows the antibacterial rate of 87.15%. Besides, the PEC platform exhibits obviously enhanced VLD photocatalytic antibacterial activity (99.99%) compared with pure BiOI. The phenomenon is mainly attributed to that AuNPs can enhance the light absorbing (see Fig. S7 in the ESI) and promote the separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs [41].

In summary, a PEC platform integrated with multifunction including capture, detection, and inactivation of pathogenic bacteria was successfully fabricated in this paper. Based on the capture ability of boronic acid group and enhanced VLD photocatalytic properties of AuNPs/BiOI, the PEC platform presents low detection limit of 46 cfu/mL and high antibacterial activity (99.99%) within 30 min. Moreover, the PEC platform can open newer promising multifunctional platforms for bacteria detection in environment protection and public health.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Natured Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 41476068 and 41776090), the National Key Research and Development Program of China (Grant No. 2016YFC1400605), the National Key Basic Research Program of China (Grant No. 2014CB643304), Science & Technology Basic Research Program of Qingdao (16-5-1-19-jch) and AoShan Talent Program Supported by Qingdao National Laboratory for Marine Science and Technology.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2018.07.153.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Detecting bacteria and determining their susceptibility to antibiotics by stochastic confinement in nanoliter droplets using plug-based microfluidics

Simple bead assay for detection of live bacteria (Escherichia coli)
One-step synthesis of biofunctional carbon quantum dots for bacterial labeling

A review of conventional detection and enumeration methods for pathogenic bacteria in food

Efficient capture, rapid killing and ultrasensitive detection of bacteria by a nano-decorated multi-functional electrode sensor
A synergistic capture strategy for enhanced detection and elimination of bacteria
A novel multifunctional electrochemical platform for simultaneous detection, elimination, and inactivation of pathogenic bacteria based on the vancomycin-functionalised AgNPs/3D-ZnO nanorod arrays
Photocatalytic inactivation of Escherischia coli: effect of concentration of TiO2 and microorganism, nature, and intensity of UV irradiation
Antimicrobial properties of highly efficient photocatalytic TiO2 nanotubes
Increasing solar absorption for photocatalysis with black hydrogenated titanium dioxide nanocrystals
Antibacterial activity of graphene Oxide/g-C3N4 composite through photocatalytic disinfection under visible light
Facile synthesis of silver sulfide/bismuth sulfide nanocomposites for photocatalytic inactivation of Escherichia coli under solar light irradiation
Engineering BiOX (X = Cl, Br, I) nanostructures for highly efficient photocatalytic applications
Synthesis of magnetically separable Ag3PO4/TiO2/Fe3O4 heterostructure with enhanced photocatalytic performance under visible light for photoinactivation of bacteria
Enhancing visible light photocatalytic degradation performance and bactericidal activity of BiOI via ultrathin-layer structure
Spray pyrolysis deposition and photoelectrochemical properties of n-type BiOI nanoplatelet thin films
Fabrication of a novel bifunctional material of BiOI/Ag3VO4 with high adsorption-photocatalysis for efficient treatment of dye wastewater
Highly sensitive visible light activated photoelectrochemical biosensing of organophosphate pesticide using biofunctional crossed bismuth oxyiodide flake arrays
DNA methyltransferase activity assay based on visible light-activated photoelectrochemical biosensor
Bismuth oxyiodide couples with glucose oxidase: a special synergized dual-catalysis mechanism for photoelectrochemical enzymatic bioanalysis
Nitrogen functionlized graphene quantum dots/3D bismuth oxyiodine hybrid hollow microspheres as remarkable photoelectrode for photoelectrochemical sensing of chlopyrifos
Facile in situ growth of high strong BiOI network films on metal wire meshes with photocatalytic activity
A reusable interface constructed by 3-aminophenylboronic acid-functionalized multiwalled carbon nanotubes for cell capture, release, and cytosensing
Boronic acid functionalized photosensitizers: a strategy to target the surface of Bacteria and implement active agents in polymer coatings
Human erythrocyte bisphosphoglycerate mutase: inactivation by glycation in vivo and in vitro
A novel multicomponent redox polymer nanobead based high performance non-enzymatic glucose sensor
Synthesis-modification integration: one-step fabrication of boronic acid functionalized carbon dots for fluorescent blood sugar sensing
Enrichment and analysis of nonenzymatically glycated peptides: boronate affinity chromatography coupled with electron-transfer dissociation mass spectrometry
Stationary phase-based two-dimensional chromatography combining both covalent and noncovalent interactions on a single HPLC column
A hierarchical charge transport cascade based onW-Bi2S3/poly(thiophenyl-3-boronic acid) hybrid for robustphotoelectrochemical analysis of subgroup J of avian leukosis virus
Simultaneous capture, detection, and inactivation of bacteria as enabled by a surface-enhanced Raman scattering multifunctional chip
Phenylboronic acid functionalized gold nanoparticles for highly sensitive detection of Staphylococcus aureus
An electrochemical assay for DNA methylation, methyltransferase activity and inhibitor screening based on methyl binding dom ain protein
Rapid water disinfection using vertically aligned MoS2 nanofilms and visible light
Synthesis and enhanced visible-light photoelectrocatalytic activity of p−n junction BiOI/TiO2 nanotube arrays
Visible light photocatalysis of BiOI and its photocatalytic activity enhancement by in situ ionic liquid modification
Chemical etching preparation of the Bi2WO6 /BiOI p – n heterojunction with enhanced photocatalytic antifouling activity under visible light irradiation
Highly stabilized nucleotide-capped small gold nanoparticles with tunable size
Enhanced photocatalytic disinfection of E. Coli 8099 using Ag/BiOI composite under visible light irradiation

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Zhiqing Yang received his master’s degree in applied chemistry from College of Chemistry and Material Science, Shandong Agricultural University in 2016. He is currently a doctoral candidate in Key Laboratory of Marine Environmental Corrosion and Bio-fouling, Institute of Oceanology, Chinese Academy of Science and his research interest is bioelectroanalysis and marine corrosive bacteria detection.

Yi Wang is an associate professor in Institute of Oceanology, Chinese Academy of Sciences. He received his master degree (applied chemistry) and doctor degree (applied chemistry) from College of Science, Beijing University of Chemical Technology in 2004 and in 2009, respectively. His current research interests include preparation of nano-functional antifouling material and new method for environmental pollutant control.

Du Zhang earned her BS degree in metal material in 1987 from Beijing Institute of Technology, China, MS degree in metal material in 1993 from Institute of Metal Research, Chinese Academy of Sciences, China, and PhD in material electrochemistry from Tokyo Institute of Technology, Japan under the supervision of Prof. Takeo Ohsaka in 2003. Currently, she is a research professor of Institute of Oceanology, Chinese Academy of Sciences, China. Her main research interests are marine corrosion and protection and marine corrosive bacteria detection.
